---
layout: post
title: "Algebraic Structures and Field Axioms"
date: 2026-03-26
category: maths
lang: en
lang_pair: "/blog/algebraic-structures-and-field-axioms-hindi/"
permalink: /blog/algebraic-structures-and-field-axioms-english/
description: "Binary operations, the algebraic ladder from Group to Field, all nine field axioms, canonical examples ℚ and ℝ, and key derived properties — with 4 worked examples for CSIR NET, GATE, IIT JAM."
---

<style>
  .math-post{font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.9;color:#2d2d2d;max-width:860px;margin:0 auto}
  .post-hero{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72 0%,#2a5298 50%,#7c3aed 100%);border-radius:16px;padding:38px 35px;color:white;margin-bottom:35px;position:relative;overflow:hidden}
  .post-hero::before{content:"𝔽";position:absolute;right:18px;top:0;font-size:8.5rem;color:rgba(255,255,255,0.07);font-family:serif;font-style:italic}
  .post-hero h1{font-size:1.85rem;font-weight:800;color:white;margin:0 0 10px;line-height:1.3}
  .post-hero p{font-size:1rem;opacity:.9;margin:0 0 14px}
  .lang-switch{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:8px;background:rgba(255,255,255,0.15);border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.35);border-radius:50px;padding:7px 18px;font-size:.85rem;color:white;text-decoration:none;transition:background .2s}
  .lang-switch:hover{background:rgba(255,255,255,0.28);color:white;text-decoration:none}
  .stats-strip{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72,#7c3aed);border-radius:14px;padding:22px 28px;display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;justify-content:space-around;gap:16px;margin-bottom:35px;text-align:center;color:white}
  .stat-item{display:flex;flex-direction:column;gap:4px;min-width:120px}
  .stat-val{font-size:1.6rem;font-weight:800}
  .stat-lbl{font-size:.78rem;opacity:.82;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.04em}
  .section-title{display:flex;align-items:center;gap:12px;margin:38px 0 18px;border-bottom:2px solid;border-image:linear-gradient(90deg,#1e3c72,transparent) 1;padding-bottom:6px}
  .sec-icon{width:38px;height:38px;border-radius:10px;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;font-size:1.15rem;flex-shrink:0}
  .section-title h2{font-size:1.35rem;font-weight:800;margin:0;background:linear-gradient(90deg,#1e3c72,#7c3aed);-webkit-background-clip:text;-webkit-text-fill-color:transparent}
  .info-card{background:white;border-radius:12px;padding:22px 26px;margin:18px 0;box-shadow:0 2px 12px rgba(0,0,0,.08)}
  .info-card.purple{border-left:5px solid #7c3aed;background:#f5f3ff}
  .info-card.gold{border-left:5px solid #d97706;background:#fffbeb}
  .info-card.green{border-left:5px solid #059669;background:#ecfdf5}
  .info-card.hindi{border-left:5px solid #d97706;background:#fefce8;font-family:'Hind','Noto Sans Devanagari',sans-serif}
  .info-card-header{font-size:.75rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.08em;margin-bottom:10px}
  .info-card-header.purple{color:#7c3aed}
  .info-card-header.gold{color:#d97706}
  .info-card-header.green{color:#059669}
  .ladder{display:flex;flex-direction:column;gap:0;margin:20px 0}
  .ladder-step{display:flex;align-items:center;gap:14px;padding:11px 18px;border-radius:8px;position:relative}
  .ladder-step::after{content:"↑";position:absolute;left:28px;bottom:-14px;font-size:.9rem;color:#94a3b8;font-weight:700}
  .ladder-step:last-child::after{display:none}
  .ladder-badge{min-width:42px;height:28px;border-radius:6px;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;font-size:.72rem;font-weight:800;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.04em;color:white;flex-shrink:0}
  .ladder-name{font-weight:700;font-size:.95rem;min-width:140px}
  .ladder-req{font-size:.88rem;color:#475569}
  .axiom-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:14px;margin:18px 0}
  .axiom-block{background:white;border-radius:10px;padding:16px 18px;border-top:4px solid;box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,.07)}
  .axiom-block h4{font-size:.82rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.05em;margin:0 0 10px}
  .axiom-row{display:flex;gap:10px;align-items:flex-start;margin:4px 0;font-size:.91rem;line-height:1.55}
  .axiom-label{font-weight:700;color:#0f172a;min-width:40px;font-size:.8rem;flex-shrink:0}
  .example-card{background:white;border-radius:12px;padding:24px 26px;margin:22px 0;border-top:5px solid;box-shadow:0 2px 12px rgba(0,0,0,.08);transition:transform .2s,box-shadow .2s}
  .example-card:hover{transform:translateY(-2px);box-shadow:0 6px 22px rgba(0,0,0,.12)}
  .example-card.ex1{border-top-color:#059669}
  .example-card.ex2{border-top-color:#2a5298}
  .example-card.ex3{border-top-color:#7c3aed}
  .example-card.ex4{border-top-color:#dc2626}
  .example-header{display:flex;align-items:center;gap:14px;margin-bottom:16px}
  .example-num{width:36px;height:36px;border-radius:50%;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;color:white;font-weight:800;font-size:1rem;flex-shrink:0}
  .ex1 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#059669,#34d399)}
  .ex2 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#2a5298,#60a5fa)}
  .ex3 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#7c3aed,#a78bfa)}
  .ex4 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#dc2626,#f87171)}
  .example-label{font-size:.72rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.08em;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:20px}
  .ex1 .example-label{background:#d1fae5;color:#059669}
  .ex2 .example-label{background:#dbeafe;color:#2a5298}
  .ex3 .example-label{background:#ede9fe;color:#7c3aed}
  .ex4 .example-label{background:#fee2e2;color:#dc2626}
  .example-title{font-weight:700;font-size:1rem;color:#0f172a}
  .solution-block{background:#ecfdf5;border-left:4px solid #059669;border-radius:0 10px 10px 0;padding:16px 20px;margin-top:14px}
  .solution-block p{margin:6px 0}
  .proof-block{background:#f8fafc;border-left:4px solid #94a3b8;border-radius:0 10px 10px 0;padding:14px 18px;margin-top:10px;font-style:italic;font-size:.93rem}
  .proof-block p{margin:5px 0}
  .warning-box{background:#fef2f2;border-left:5px solid #dc2626;border-radius:0 10px 10px 0;padding:20px 24px;margin:18px 0}
  .warning-box h4{color:#dc2626;margin:0 0 12px;font-size:1rem}
  .warning-item{margin:12px 0;padding-left:14px;border-left:2px solid #fca5a5}
  .warning-label{font-weight:700;color:#dc2626;font-size:.88rem}
  .revision-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(3,1fr);gap:18px;margin:22px 0}
  .rev-card{border-radius:12px;padding:20px 18px;color:white}
  .rev-card.a{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72,#2a5298)}
  .rev-card.b{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#7c3aed,#a78bfa)}
  .rev-card.c{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#d97706,#f59e0b)}
  .rev-card h4{font-size:.88rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.06em;margin:0 0 12px;opacity:.9}
  .rev-card ul{margin:0;padding-left:16px;font-size:.9rem;line-height:1.75}
  .app-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fit,minmax(185px,1fr));gap:16px;margin:22px 0}
  .app-card{background:white;border-radius:12px;padding:20px 18px;border-top:4px solid;box-shadow:0 2px 10px rgba(0,0,0,.07);transition:transform .2s}
  .app-card:hover{transform:translateY(-3px)}
  .app-card h4{font-size:.92rem;font-weight:700;margin:8px 0 6px;color:#0f172a}
  .app-card p{font-size:.85rem;color:#475569;margin:0;line-height:1.55}
  .app-icon{font-size:1.6rem}
  .summary-table{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;margin:18px 0;font-size:.92rem}
  .summary-table th{background:#1e3c72;color:white;padding:11px 14px;text-align:left;font-size:.82rem;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.05em}
  .summary-table td{padding:10px 14px;border-bottom:1px solid #e2e8f0;vertical-align:top}
  .summary-table tr:nth-child(even) td{background:#f8fafc}
  .highlight-result{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72,#7c3aed);border-radius:14px;padding:26px 30px;text-align:center;color:white;margin:28px 0}
  .highlight-result p{margin:0;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.75}
  .social-footer{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#0f172a,#1e3c72);color:white;border-radius:14px;padding:28px 30px;margin:40px 0 20px;text-align:center}
  .social-footer a{color:#93c5fd}
  .social-footer p{margin:8px 0}
  .pdf-section{text-align:center;margin:44px 0 28px}
  .pdf-btn{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:14px;background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1B2A4A,#2A5298);color:white;padding:15px 36px;border-radius:6px;font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1rem;font-weight:700;box-shadow:0 4px 18px rgba(27,42,74,.40);transition:transform .2s,box-shadow .2s;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn:hover{transform:translateY(-3px);box-shadow:0 8px 26px rgba(27,42,74,.50);color:white;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn .pi{font-size:1.5rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt{display:flex;flex-direction:column;text-align:left;line-height:1.35}
  .pdf-btn .pt1{font-size:1rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt2{font-size:.72rem;opacity:.82;font-weight:400}
  @media(max-width:700px){.axiom-grid{grid-template-columns:1fr}.revision-grid{grid-template-columns:1fr}}
</style>

<div class="math-post">

<!-- §1 HERO + STATS -->
<div class="post-hero">
  <h1>Algebraic Structures and Field Axioms</h1>
  <p>Every time you add fractions, solve a linear equation, or take a square root, you are implicitly using the axioms of a field. This post builds the concept from first principles: we climb the algebraic ladder from binary operations to groups, rings, integral domains, and finally fields — writing down all nine axioms explicitly and proving the key consequences that follow from them.</p>
  <a class="lang-switch" href="/blog/algebraic-structures-and-field-axioms-hindi/">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में पढ़ें</a>
</div>

<div class="stats-strip">
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">9</span><span class="stat-lbl">Field Axioms</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">4</span><span class="stat-lbl">Algebraic levels</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">ℚ, ℝ, 𝔽ₚ</span><span class="stat-lbl">Canonical Fields</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">4</span><span class="stat-lbl">Solved Examples</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">CSIR/GATE/JAM</span><span class="stat-lbl">Exam Relevance</span></div>
</div>

<!-- §2 DEFINITION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ede9fe;">&#x1D53D;</div>
  <h2>Binary Operations and the Algebraic Ladder</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Binary Operation</div>
  <p>A <strong>binary operation</strong> on a non-empty set $S$ is a function $* \colon S \times S \to S$. We write $a * b$ for $*(a,b)$. The set $S$ is automatically <strong>closed</strong> under $*$ by this definition.</p>
</div>

<div class="ladder">
  <div class="ladder-step" style="background:#f0fdf4;">
    <div class="ladder-badge" style="background:#059669;">Field</div>
    <div class="ladder-name" style="color:#059669;">Field</div>
    <div class="ladder-req">Integral Domain + every $x \neq 0$ has $x^{-1}$</div>
  </div>
  <div class="ladder-step" style="background:#eff6ff;">
    <div class="ladder-badge" style="background:#2a5298;">Int.Dom</div>
    <div class="ladder-name" style="color:#2a5298;">Integral Domain</div>
    <div class="ladder-req">Comm. Ring with unity $1\neq0$ + no zero divisors</div>
  </div>
  <div class="ladder-step" style="background:#faf5ff;">
    <div class="ladder-badge" style="background:#7c3aed;">CRing</div>
    <div class="ladder-name" style="color:#7c3aed;">Commutative Ring</div>
    <div class="ladder-req">Ring + multiplication commutative</div>
  </div>
  <div class="ladder-step" style="background:#fffbeb;">
    <div class="ladder-badge" style="background:#d97706;">Ring</div>
    <div class="ladder-name" style="color:#d97706;">Ring</div>
    <div class="ladder-req">$(S,+)$ abelian group, $(S,\cdot)$ semigroup, distributive</div>
  </div>
  <div class="ladder-step" style="background:#fef2f2;">
    <div class="ladder-badge" style="background:#dc2626;">Group</div>
    <div class="ladder-name" style="color:#dc2626;">Group</div>
    <div class="ladder-req">Closed, associative, identity, inverses (+commutative = Abelian)</div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; The Nine Field Axioms — Definition 1.12 (Rudin)</div>
  <p>A <strong>field</strong> is a set $F$ with two binary operations $+$ and $\cdot$ satisfying:</p>
  <div class="axiom-grid">
    <div class="axiom-block" style="border-top-color:#2a5298;">
      <h4 style="color:#2a5298;">&#x2795; Addition (A1–A4)</h4>
      <div class="axiom-row"><span class="axiom-label">(A1)</span><span><em>Comm.:</em> $x+y=y+x$</span></div>
      <div class="axiom-row"><span class="axiom-label">(A2)</span><span><em>Assoc.:</em> $(x+y)+z=x+(y+z)$</span></div>
      <div class="axiom-row"><span class="axiom-label">(A3)</span><span><em>Identity:</em> $\exists\,0\in F$: $0+x=x$</span></div>
      <div class="axiom-row"><span class="axiom-label">(A4)</span><span><em>Inverse:</em> $\exists\,{-x}$: $x+(-x)=0$</span></div>
    </div>
    <div class="axiom-block" style="border-top-color:#7c3aed;">
      <h4 style="color:#7c3aed;">&#x2716; Multiplication (M1–M4)</h4>
      <div class="axiom-row"><span class="axiom-label">(M1)</span><span><em>Comm.:</em> $xy=yx$</span></div>
      <div class="axiom-row"><span class="axiom-label">(M2)</span><span><em>Assoc.:</em> $(xy)z=x(yz)$</span></div>
      <div class="axiom-row"><span class="axiom-label">(M3)</span><span><em>Identity:</em> $\exists\,1\neq0$: $1\cdot x=x$</span></div>
      <div class="axiom-row"><span class="axiom-label">(M4)</span><span><em>Inverse:</em> $x\neq0\Rightarrow\exists\,x^{-1}$: $xx^{-1}=1$</span></div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="axiom-block" style="border-top-color:#d97706;margin-top:10px;">
    <h4 style="color:#d97706;">&#x1F4CF; Distributive Law (D)</h4>
    <div class="axiom-row"><span class="axiom-label">(D)</span><span>$x(y+z)=xy+xz$ for all $x,y,z\in F$</span></div>
  </div>
  <p style="margin-top:14px;"><strong>Canonical fields:</strong> $\mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{R}$, $\mathbb{C}$, $\mathbb{F}_p = \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ (prime $p$).</p>
  <p><strong>Non-example:</strong> $\mathbb{Z}$ fails (M4): $2 \in \mathbb{Z}$ has no multiplicative inverse in $\mathbb{Z}$. So $\mathbb{Z}$ is an integral domain, not a field.</p>
</div>

📖 <strong>Reference:</strong> Rudin, W., <strong>Principles of Mathematical Analysis</strong>, 3rd ed., Ch. 1, Definitions 1.12 & 1.17. Also: Apostol, T.M., <strong>Mathematical Analysis</strong>, 2nd ed., Ch. 1, §1.3–1.4.

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F517; Prerequisites</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/sets-and-basic-notation-english/"><strong>Sets and Basic Notation</strong></a> — carrier sets of algebraic structures.<br>
  <strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods</strong></a> — direct proof and contradiction used throughout derived-property proofs.<br>
  <strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/functions-and-relations-english/"><strong>Functions and Relations</strong></a> — binary operations are functions $A \times A \to A$.</p>
</div>

<!-- §3 INTUITION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#d1fae5;">&#x1F4A1;</div>
  <h2>Intuition — What a Field Really Is</h2>
</div>

<p>A field is the <strong>minimal algebraic setting in which all four arithmetic operations — addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division (by non-zero elements) — are well-defined and satisfy the rules you learned in school</strong>. The nine axioms are simply those school rules, written precisely.</p>

<div class="info-card green">
  <div class="info-card-header green">&#x1F50D; The Critical Axiom: (M4)</div>
  <p>The single axiom that separates a <em>field</em> from a mere <em>ring</em> or <em>integral domain</em> is <strong>(M4) — every non-zero element has a multiplicative inverse</strong>. This is what makes division possible. In $\mathbb{Z}$, the element $2$ has no inverse (since $\tfrac{1}{2} \notin \mathbb{Z}$), so $\mathbb{Z}$ is not a field. In $\mathbb{Q}$ and $\mathbb{R}$, every non-zero element $a$ has inverse $\tfrac{1}{a}$, so both are fields.</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Derived Properties (Rudin, Prop. 1.14)</div>
  <p>From just the nine axioms, one can prove all the following for any field $F$ and $a,b \in F$:</p>
  <ul style="margin:10px 0;padding-left:20px;line-height:1.9">
    <li><strong>Uniqueness of $0$ and $1$:</strong> If $a+b=a$ then $b=0$; if $ab=a$ ($a\neq0$) then $b=1$.</li>
    <li><strong>Double negative:</strong> $-(-a)=a$.</li>
    <li><strong>Zero product:</strong> $a \cdot 0 = 0$ for all $a$.</li>
    <li><strong>Sign rules:</strong> $(-a)b = -(ab)$; in particular $(-a)(-b)=ab$.</li>
    <li><strong>No zero divisors:</strong> $ab=0 \Rightarrow a=0$ or $b=0$.</li>
    <li><strong>Cancellation:</strong> $a+b=a+c \Rightarrow b=c$; &nbsp; $ab=ac$, $a\neq0 \Rightarrow b=c$.</li>
  </ul>
</div>

<div class="info-card hindi">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में संक्षेप</div>
  <p><strong>द्विआधारी संक्रिया (Binary Operation)</strong> $*\colon S\times S\to S$ एक फलन है। बीजगणितीय संरचनाओं का सोपान: Group → Ring → Integral Domain → <strong>Field (क्षेत्र)</strong>। एक <strong>Field</strong> में 9 अभिगृहीत होते हैं: (A1–A4) योग के लिए, (M1–M4) गुणन के लिए, (D) वितरण नियम। महत्त्वपूर्ण अभिगृहीत (M4): प्रत्येक अशून्य अवयव का गुणात्मक प्रतिलोम होता है — यही Field को Ring से अलग करता है। $\mathbb{Q}$ और $\mathbb{R}$ Field हैं; $\mathbb{Z}$ Field नहीं (क्योंकि $2^{-1}=\frac{1}{2}\notin\mathbb{Z}$)।</p>
</div>

<!-- §4 SOLVED EXAMPLES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x270F;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Solved Examples</h2>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex1">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">1</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Very Easy &nbsp;|&nbsp; Verifying an Axiom</div>
      <div class="example-title">Show axiom (A4) holds for $x = \tfrac{3}{5} \in \mathbb{Q}$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <p>Exhibit the additive inverse of $\tfrac{3}{5}$ in $\mathbb{Q}$ and verify it satisfies (A4).</p>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Additive inverse needed:</strong> We need $y \in \mathbb{Q}$ such that $\tfrac{3}{5} + y = 0$.</p>
    <p><strong>Candidate:</strong> Take $y = -\tfrac{3}{5} \in \mathbb{Q}$.</p>
    <p><strong>Verification:</strong> $\dfrac{3}{5} + \left(-\dfrac{3}{5}\right) = \dfrac{3-3}{5} = \dfrac{0}{5} = 0$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p>Axiom (A4) holds. Every rational number has an additive inverse in $\mathbb{Q}$, confirming $(\mathbb{Q},+,\cdot)$ satisfies (A4). $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex2">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">2</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Easy–Medium &nbsp;|&nbsp; Derived Property from Axioms</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove $a \cdot 0 = 0$ for every $a$ in a field $F$, citing axioms at each step</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Step 1.</strong> By (A3): $0 + 0 = 0$.</p>
    <p><strong>Step 2.</strong> Multiply both sides by $a$: $a(0+0) = a \cdot 0$.</p>
    <p><strong>Step 3.</strong> By (D): $a \cdot 0 + a \cdot 0 = a \cdot 0$.</p>
    <p><strong>Step 4.</strong> Add $-(a\cdot 0)$ to both sides (by A4):<br>
    $a\cdot 0 + (a\cdot 0 + (-(a\cdot 0))) = a\cdot 0 + (-(a\cdot 0))$<br>
    $a\cdot 0 + 0 = 0$ (by A4, A2, A3), so $a\cdot 0 = 0$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex3">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">3</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Medium–Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; Proving a Non-Example</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove rigorously that $(\mathbb{Z}, +, \cdot)$ is NOT a field</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Strategy:</strong> Show axiom (M4) fails for some nonzero element.</p>
    <p><strong>Candidate:</strong> Take $a = 2 \in \mathbb{Z}$, $a \neq 0$.</p>
    <p><strong>Suppose</strong> (M4) holds for $a=2$: then $\exists\, b \in \mathbb{Z}$ with $2b=1$, i.e., $b = \tfrac{1}{2}$. But $\tfrac{1}{2} \notin \mathbb{Z}$. Contradiction.</p>
    <p><strong>General argument:</strong> For any $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ with $|n| \geq 2$, if $nb=1$ for some $b \in \mathbb{Z}$, then $n \mid 1$ in $\mathbb{Z}$, forcing $n = \pm 1$ — a contradiction. So (M4) fails for all $n$ with $|n| \geq 2$.</p>
    <p>Hence $(\mathbb{Z},+,\cdot)$ is not a field. (It is an integral domain, satisfying all axioms except M4.) $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex4">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">4</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; CSIR NET / GATE / IIT JAM Level</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove: (i) $(-a)(-b)=ab$; (ii) $ab=0 \Rightarrow a=0$ or $b=0$, using only field axioms</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Part (i): $(-a)(-b) = ab$.</strong></p>
    <div class="proof-block">
      <p>First show $(-a)b = -(ab)$: $\;ab + (-a)b = (a+(-a))b = 0\cdot b = 0$ (by D, A4, then $0\cdot b=0$ from Example 2). So $(-a)b$ is the additive inverse of $ab$, i.e. $(-a)b=-(ab)$.</p>
      <p>Apply with $b$ replaced by $-b$: $(-a)(-b)=-(a(-b))=-(-(ab))=ab$ (using $-(-ab)=ab$ from A4 and uniqueness). $\blacksquare$</p>
    </div>
    <p><strong>Part (ii): $ab=0 \Rightarrow a=0$ or $b=0$.</strong></p>
    <div class="proof-block">
      <p>Suppose $ab=0$ and $a \neq 0$. Since $F$ is a field, $a^{-1}$ exists by (M4). Multiply $ab=0$ by $a^{-1}$: $a^{-1}(ab) = a^{-1}\cdot 0$. LHS $= (a^{-1}a)b = 1\cdot b = b$ (by M2, M4, M3). RHS $= 0$ (by Example 2). Hence $b=0$. $\blacksquare$</p>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §5 QUICK REVISION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#dbeafe;">&#x26A1;</div>
  <h2>Quick Revision Cards</h2>
</div>

<div class="revision-grid">
  <div class="rev-card a">
    <h4>&#x1F4CA; A — The Nine Axioms</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>(A1) $x+y=y+x$</li>
      <li>(A2) $(x+y)+z=x+(y+z)$</li>
      <li>(A3) $\exists 0$: $0+x=x$</li>
      <li>(A4) $\exists{-x}$: $x+(-x)=0$</li>
      <li>(M1–M4) same structure for $\cdot$; (M3): $1\neq0$</li>
      <li>(D) $x(y+z)=xy+xz$</li>
      <li>Derived: $a\cdot0=0$; $(-a)(-b)=ab$; no zero divisors</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card b">
    <h4>&#x2699;&#xFE0F; B — Edge Cases &amp; Conditions</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>(M4) only for $x \neq 0$; $0$ has no multiplicative inverse</li>
      <li>$1 \neq 0$ required; $\{0\}$ alone is NOT a field</li>
      <li>$\mathbb{Z}$: integral domain, NOT a field</li>
      <li>$\mathbb{Q}, \mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C}, \mathbb{F}_p$: all fields</li>
      <li>Field ⊂ Integral Domain ⊂ Ring (each set is proper)</li>
      <li>$-a$ = additive inverse; $a^{-1}$ = multiplicative inverse</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card c">
    <h4>&#x1F3AF; C — Exam Tips</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>&#x1F535; <strong>CSIR NET:</strong> To disprove "is a field": find one $x\neq0$ with no inverse</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E2; <strong>GATE:</strong> Cite axiom label at every proof step</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E0; <strong>IIT JAM:</strong> Know Ring ⊃ Int. Domain ⊃ Field with examples</li>
      <li>&#x1F534; <strong>B.Sc. Raj.:</strong> Verify all 9 axioms when showing something IS a field</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §6 COMMON MISTAKES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fee2e2;">&#x26A0;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Common Mistakes</h2>
</div>

<div class="warning-box">
  <h4>&#x274C; Errors to Avoid</h4>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 1: Claiming ℤ is a field</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "$\mathbb{Z}$ satisfies all usual arithmetic rules, so it is a field." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> $\mathbb{Z}$ fails (M4): $2 \in \mathbb{Z}$ has no multiplicative inverse in $\mathbb{Z}$. $\mathbb{Z}$ is only an integral domain.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 2: Forgetting $1 \neq 0$ in (M3)</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> Accepting $\{0\}$ with $0 \cdot 0 = 0$ and $0+0=0$ as a field. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> (M3) explicitly requires $1 \neq 0$. Without this, the trivial set $\{0\}$ (where $0=1$) would satisfy all other axioms.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 3: Applying (M4) to $0$</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "Every element has a multiplicative inverse, so $0^{-1}$ exists." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> (M4) applies only to <em>non-zero</em> elements. Since $0 \cdot b = 0 \neq 1$ for all $b$, $0$ never has an inverse in any field.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 4: Confusing $-a$ with $a^{-1}$</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> Writing $a^{-1}$ when $-a$ is meant, or vice versa. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> $-a$ is the <em>additive</em> inverse: $a + (-a) = 0$. $a^{-1}$ is the <em>multiplicative</em> inverse: $a \cdot a^{-1} = 1$ (requires $a \neq 0$). They are entirely different objects.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §7 APPLICATIONS -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ecfdf5;">&#x1F310;</div>
  <h2>Real-World Applications</h2>
</div>

<div class="app-grid">
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#2a5298;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F512;</div>
    <h4>Cryptography — AES</h4>
    <p>AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) operates over $\mathbb{F}_{2^8}$, a finite field with 256 elements. Addition is XOR; (M4) guarantees every encryption step is invertible.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#059669;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4F1;</div>
    <h4>Error-Correcting Codes</h4>
    <p>Reed–Solomon codes (QR codes, CDs, deep-space signals) are built over finite fields. The field structure uniquely determines which errors can be corrected and how.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#7c3aed;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x269B;&#xFE0F;</div>
    <h4>Quantum Mechanics</h4>
    <p>Quantum state spaces are vector spaces over $\mathbb{C}$, a field. All observable quantities and unitary transformations are built on $\mathbb{C}$ satisfying the field axioms.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#d97706;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F9EE;</div>
    <h4>Galois Theory</h4>
    <p>The unsolvability of the general quintic equation is proved by studying field extensions. Every step in Galois's proof relies on the nine axioms of a field.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §8 SUMMARY TABLE -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#e0e7ff;">&#x1F4CB;</div>
  <h2>Summary Table &amp; Key Result</h2>
</div>

<table class="summary-table">
  <thead>
    <tr><th>Structure</th><th>Key extra requirement</th><th>Example</th><th>Non-example</th></tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr><td><strong>Group</strong></td><td>Assoc., identity, inverses (+comm. = Abelian)</td><td>$(\mathbb{Z},+)$</td><td>$(\mathbb{N},+)$</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Ring</strong></td><td>Two ops, distributive law</td><td>$(\mathbb{Z},+,\cdot)$</td><td>$(\mathbb{N},+,\cdot)$</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Integral Domain</strong></td><td>No zero divisors, $1\neq0$</td><td>$\mathbb{Z}$</td><td>$\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Field</strong></td><td>(M4): $x\neq0 \Rightarrow x^{-1}$ exists</td><td>$\mathbb{Q},\mathbb{R},\mathbb{F}_p$</td><td>$\mathbb{Z}$</td></tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<div class="highlight-result">
  <p><strong>The Algebraic Hierarchy:</strong></p>
  <p>$$\text{Group} \;\supset\; \text{Ring} \;\supset\; \text{Integral Domain} \;\supset\; \text{Field}$$</p>
  <p>A field $(F,+,\cdot)$ satisfies all nine axioms (A1–A4, M1–M4, D).<br>
  Key derived fact: $\;ab=0 \Rightarrow a=0 \text{ or } b=0$ (no zero divisors).<br>
  $\mathbb{Q} \subsetneq \mathbb{R} \subsetneq \mathbb{C}$ — each is a field.</p>
</div>

<!-- §9 CROSS-REFERENCES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x1F517;</div>
  <h2>Cross-References &amp; Related Posts</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F4DA; Prerequisites &amp; Next Steps</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190; Prerequisites:</strong>
    <a href="/blog/sets-and-basic-notation-english/"><strong>Sets and Basic Notation</strong></a> — carrier sets of algebraic structures &nbsp;|&nbsp;
    <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods</strong></a> — direct proof and contradiction &nbsp;|&nbsp;
    <a href="/blog/functions-and-relations-english/"><strong>Functions and Relations</strong></a> — binary operations as functions.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x2192; Next Topic:</strong> <em>The Real Number Field and Ordered Fields</em> — adding an order relation to the field axioms; the Least Upper Bound property that distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x1F4D6; Further Reading:</strong> Rudin, Ch. 1, §§1.12–1.38; Apostol, Ch. 1, §§1.3–1.6; Bartle &amp; Sherbert, Ch. 2, §2.1.</p>
</div>
